response:
# Menu Button with Dropdown Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (JAWS, NVDA), keyboard-only users, low-vision users (focus visibility), users with motor impairments (target size, no time limits) > **Assistive technologies:** JAWS + Chrome, JAWS + Firefox, NVDA + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, keyboard-only (no AT) **Feature:** A reusable React menu button that toggles a dropdown of 5–8 actionable menu items, navigable by mouse and keyboard, with some items optionally disabled and optional nested submenus. **Risk Level:** High — this is a composite widget with focus movement, roving tabindex, a non-obvious "focus moves into the menu" model, disabled-item skip logic, and screen-reader role semantics that automated tools cannot fully validate. **Component Type:** Menu Button (actions menu) → APG Menu Button pattern. --- ## Scope & Context **What is being built?** A `MenuButton` React component: a trigger button labeled "Menu" or "Actions" that opens a dropdown list of 5–8 menu items. Items are activated by click or keyboard; Escape closes the menu and returns focus to the button. Some items may be `disabled`; nested submenus are an optional second phase. **What user need does it address?** A compact, discoverable way to expose a set of actions (e.g., row actions, account actions) without consuming permanent screen space, usable identically by mouse, keyboard, and screen reader users. **Who needs accessibility?** - **Keyboard-only users** — must open, traverse, activate, and dismiss the menu with no pointer; focus must be visible at every step and must never get stranded. - **Screen reader users (JAWS, NVDA)** — must hear the button's name, role, and expanded/collapsed state; must hear each item's name, role (menu item), position in set, and disabled state; must perceive when the menu opens and closes. - **Low-vision users** — must see a focus indicator meeting 3:1 contrast on both the button and the active item; must be able to zoom to 200% (and reflow at 400%) without losing the menu. - **Motor-impaired users** — 24×24 (ideally 44×44) CSS px targets; no hover-only affordance; no timing dependency. **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA. This pattern specifically engages 1.3.1, 1.4.1, 1.4.3, 1.4.11, 1.4.13, 2.1.1, 2.1.2, 2.4.3, 2.4.7, 2.4.11, 2.5.8, 4.1.2, 4.1.3. **Assistive technologies to support:** JAWS and NVDA on both Chrome and Firefox; keyboard-only with no AT. VoiceOver/Safari is not in the stated target but the APG semantics used here are the same — do not introduce Mac-hostile patterns. **Risk level: High.** Justification: the menu button is one of the most commonly *mis*-built ARIA patterns. The two highest-risk decisions are (1) the focus model — a menu is **NOT a focus trap like a modal**; focus *moves into* the menu and the items themselves are not in the page Tab sequence (roving tabindex), and (2) **disabled items remain focusable** in a menu (unlike disabled form controls) so they can be discovered by screen reader users. Getting either wrong silently breaks the experience in a way axe-core will not catch. **What existing code does this modify/extend?** None — new component. There is therefore no `file:line` current-state to cite; all decisions below are greenfield design. **Constraints:** - React application — favors **conditional rendering** of the menu (mount on open, unmount on close) as the hidden-content strategy, which sidesteps the "`aria-hidden` does not remove from tab order" trap entirely. - Some items may be **disabled** → `aria-disabled="true"`, NOT the HTML `disabled` attribute (we are not using `
Edit
Duplicate
Archive
Delete
``` Exactly **one** item carries `tabindex="0"` at a time (the "active" item); all others are `tabindex="-1"`. See Focus Management. --- ## Interaction Pattern Design **Primary APG pattern:** **Menu Button** — https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/menu-button/ **Composite navigation semantics borrowed from:** **Menu and Menubar** — https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/menubar/ (for in-menu arrow navigation, type-ahead, and submenu behavior). ### Core focus model (the crux — read before anything else) A menu button is **NOT a modal dialog**. There is **no focus trap**. Instead: - When the menu opens, **focus moves off the button and into the menu** (onto a menu item). The page Tab sequence does not include the individual items — they are reached only with **Arrow keys** via a **roving tabindex** (APG: "Menu and Menubar" → Keyboard Interaction). - **Tab / Shift+Tab while the menu is open CLOSES the menu** and moves focus to the next/previous element in the page tab order (per APG Menu Button: "Tab: Moves focus to the next element in the tab sequence, and ... closes the menu."). This is the opposite of a modal's trap-and-cycle behavior. Implementing a modal-style focus trap here is a **defect**, not a safety net. This single distinction is the highest-value thing in this plan. Document it in the component's code comments too. ### Keyboard interaction model **On the button (menu closed):** | Key | Behavior | |-----|----------| | `Enter` | Opens menu; moves focus to the **first** menu item. | | `Space` | Opens menu; moves focus to the **first** menu item. | | `Down Arrow` | Opens menu; moves focus to the **first** menu item. | | `Up Arrow` | Opens menu; moves focus to the **last** menu item. (Convenience for reaching the bottom quickly.) | | `Tab` | Normal tab navigation; menu stays closed. | **Within the open menu:** | Key | Behavior | |-----|----------| | `Down Arrow` | Move focus to next item; **wraps** from last to first. Skips nothing — disabled items ARE focusable (see below). | | `Up Arrow` | Move focus to previous item; wraps from first to last. | | `Home` | Move focus to first item. | | `End` | Move focus to last item. | | `Enter` | Activate focused item's action, then close menu and return focus to button. (Disabled item: no-op — does not activate, does not close.) | | `Space` | Same as Enter for a plain `menuitem`. (For `menuitemcheckbox`/`menuitemradio` in Phase 2, Space toggles without closing.) | | `Escape` | Close menu; **return focus to the button** (WCAG 2.4.3, 2.1.2). | | `Tab` | Close menu; move focus to next focusable element in page (NOT trapped). | | `Shift+Tab` | Close menu; move focus to previous focusable element in page. | | Printable character | **Type-ahead**: move focus to the next item whose label starts with the typed character(s) (APG menu type-ahead). Optional-but-recommended for 5–8 items; strongly recommended if lists grow. | **Disabled-item decision (important, and a common bug):** Per APG, authors *may* either (a) keep disabled items **focusable** (`aria-disabled="true"`, still in the arrow-key rotation) so screen-reader users can discover that the action exists but is unavailable, or (b) skip them. **We choose (a): disabled items are focusable and announced as "dimmed/unavailable," and arrow keys land on them but Enter/Space/click do nothing.** Rationale grounded in Dead-Output rule: skipping disabled items hides information from screen-reader users (they never learn the action exists), so we keep them perceivable. This is the opposite of native form controls, where `disabled` removes the element from focus. Therefore we use `aria-disabled="true"`, **never** the HTML `disabled` attribute, on menu items. ### ARIA implementation **Button:** - `aria-haspopup="menu"` (or `"true"`) — announces "has menu / submenu available." WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value. - `aria-expanded="false"|"true"` — current open state, toggled on every open/close. WCAG 4.1.2; status conveyed per 4.1.3. - `aria-controls=""` — associates button with the menu it controls. WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships. (Note: `aria-controls` support in screen readers is inconsistent; include it for correctness, but do **not** rely on it as the *only* signal — `aria-expanded` + focus movement carry the real UX.) - Accessible name: the **visible text** "Actions"/"Menu" is the name. If the trigger is icon-only (e.g., a "⋮" kebab), add `aria-label="Actions"` (NOT `title`). Known-pitfall guard: **`title` is never the planned accessible-name mechanism** — it is unreliable across JAWS/NVDA and is mouse-only as a tooltip. **Menu container:** - `role="menu"` — WCAG 4.1.2. - `aria-labelledby=" ``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-haspopup="menu"` — WCAG 4.1.2 - `aria-expanded` (true/false) — WCAG 4.1.2, 4.1.3 - `aria-controls` (→ menu id, only while open) — WCAG 1.3.1 - accessible name via visible text, or `aria-label` if icon-only — WCAG 4.1.2; caret `aria-hidden` — WCAG 4.1.2 **Keyboard Interactions:** Enter/Space/Down → open + focus first item; Up → open + focus last item; Tab → normal (menu closed). **Tests:** button is native `

_benchmark.method: plan 006 Phase D Claude lane: Agent(subagent_type=a11y-planner, model=opus), one background subagent per fixture; verbatim PLANNER_PROMPT_PREFIX task; agent wrote plan via Write tool; orchestrator wrapped to response JSON
